---
title: "Tyler Rongxuan Chen"
image: assets/profile.jpg
about:
  template: trestles
  image-shape: rounded
  image-width: 14em
  links:
    - icon: envelope
      text: Email
      href: "mailto:tylercr@umich.edu"
    - icon: file-earmark-pdf
      text: CV
      href: assets/Tyler_Rongxuan_Chen_CV.pdf
    - icon: github
      text: GitHub
      href: "https://github.com/tylercr77"
    # TODO: uncomment and fill in once you have a Google Scholar profile URL
    # - icon: mortarboard-fill
    #   text: Google Scholar
    #   href: "https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=YOUR-ID"
    - icon: building
      text: Department Profile
      href: "https://prod.lsa.umich.edu/polisci/people/graduate-students/tyler-chen.html"
---

Tyler Rongxuan Chen is a Ph.D. candidate in Political Science and Scientific Computing at the
University of Michigan, where he is also completing an M.A. in Statistics. He studies democracy
through its parties and party systems: when and how they realign or come apart as the population
groups beneath them move, grow, shrink, and re-cluster across space and time.

His dissertation traces these scale effects across the United States, Malaysia, Taiwan, and Japan,
and his methodological work develops Bayesian, simulation-based tools — including the Bayesian
Approximate Computation with Hierarchical framework (BACH) — that recover group voting behavior
from aggregate election data when surveys fail.

His research appears in the *Journal of East Asian Studies*, and he works with primary sources in
Chinese, Malay, and Indonesian. He has taught across Michigan's quantitative methods sequence,
from the graduate statistics core to undergraduate data science, and at the Inter-university
Consortium for Political and Social Research (ICPSR) Summer Program.

He is on the 2026–27 academic job market.
